title: One dashboard query vs the checkout that pays the bills

steps.narration: 09:00. A new analyst wires a revenue dashboard straight at orders-primary — the same Postgres that every NimbusMart checkout writes to. The question looks innocent: revenue by country, plus the count of orders in the fraud-review queue (fraud_score >= FRAUD_REVIEW_THRESHOLD, the 0.80 business rule). One GROUP BY. What could it cost?

state.note: target: orders-primary (OLTP row store) · 240,000,000 rows · 31 columns · the live checkout database

steps.narration: The query needs 3 columns — country, total_amount, fraud_score — but a row store keeps every column of a row glued together on disk. To read 3 fields it drags all 31 off the platters, row by row, 240M times. There is no index that helps a full aggregate; it is a sequential heap scan.

state.note: row-oriented storage: the unit of I/O is the whole row, not the column you asked for

steps.narration: The scan holds its buffers and MVCC read snapshot for 40+ seconds. Checkout writes — the INSERT ... orders and the UPDATE ... inventory — now contend for the same pages, buffer pool, and I/O bandwidth. p99 checkout latency starts climbing off its healthy 45 ms floor.

state.note: the analytics scan and the checkout write are fighting over one machine's buffer pool

steps.narration: It gets worse downstream. The long scan pins WAL cleanup, so the read replica that feeds other services falls behind: replica lag walks from 200 ms to 38 s. Now the mobile app's "order history" shows stale data too. One report has degraded three unrelated systems.

state.note: OLTP replicas are tuned for low-latency replay, not for absorbing a 40-second analytical scan upstream

steps.narration: The business impact lands where it always does — at the till. Checkout p99 crosses the app's 3-second timeout; the payment step starts failing and retrying. During the 40 seconds the dashboard runs, roughly 7% of in-flight carts error out and a share are abandoned. A revenue report is now destroying revenue.

state.note: fulfillment impact: carts timing out at the payment step → abandoned checkouts during every dashboard refresh

steps.narration: Reset. Point the exact same question at an OLAP columnar store — a nightly Parquet snapshot of orders in the analytics warehouse. Columnar storage lays each column out contiguously, so the engine reads only the 3 columns the query touches and skips the other 28 entirely.

state.note: column-oriented storage: read only country, total_amount, fraud_score — the other 28 never leave disk

steps.narration: Same rows, same aggregate, radically different bill: reading 3 compressed columns instead of 31 raw ones cuts bytes scanned ~10×, and dictionary/run-length encoding shrinks them further. 41 s → 1.1 s. And critically, it runs on a separate cluster — the checkout database never feels it.

state.note: isolation is half the win: analytics load lives on its own engine, not on the system of record

steps.narration: With analytics moved off the primary, checkout p99 settles back to its 45 ms floor and replica lag returns to 200 ms — while the dashboard refreshes in about a second. Nobody had to choose between "the analyst gets answers" and "customers can pay."

state.note: the fulfillment line is quiet again because the workload that threatened it now lives somewhere else

steps.narration: The whole of Track A hangs on this split. OLTP is built for millions of tiny point reads and writes — row storage, B-tree indexes, row locks, one authoritative copy. OLAP is built for a few enormous scanning aggregates — columnar storage, compression, no lock contention, replicated freely. They are not the same database tuned differently; they are opposite shapes. Data engineering is, in large part, the discipline of moving data from the first shape to the second — safely, on a schedule, without ever touching the till.

state.note: OLTP = system of record (write-optimized, row store) · OLAP = system of analysis (read-optimized, columnar) · the pipeline between them is the job
